architecture.summary: kNN-LM extends a pre-trained Transformer language model by augmenting its next-word prediction with a retrieval mechanism. At inference, the model encodes the input context using an intermediate representation (the input to the final layer's feedforward network), queries a FAISS-indexed datastore of (key, value) pairs built from training data via a single forward pass, retrieves the k nearest neighbors by squared Euclidean distance, computes a softmax distribution over retrieved target tokens weighted by neighbor distances, and linearly interpolates this retrieval distribution with the base model's distribution via a tuned parameter λ.

biblio.title: Generalization through Memorization: Nearest Neighbor Language Models

claims.statement: kNN-LM achieves a new state-of-the-art perplexity of 15.79 on Wikitext-103, a 2.9 point improvement over the base Baevski & Auli (2019) model with no additional training
kNN-LM trained on Wiki-100M (100M tokens) with a datastore from Wiki-3B (3B tokens) achieves 13.73 perplexity, outperforming a model trained directly on all 3B tokens at 15.17 perplexity
kNN-LM improves perplexity on Books from 11.89 to 10.89, demonstrating effectiveness across multiple domains
Domain adaptation via in-domain datastore: adding a Books datastore to a Wiki-3B-trained model reduces perplexity from 34.84 to 20.47 (14-point improvement)
Performance monotonically improves with datastore size; a model trained on 100M tokens with 1.6B-token datastore already surpasses the performance of a model trained on all 3B tokens
kNN-LM is particularly helpful for predicting rare patterns, such as factual knowledge, names, and near-duplicate sentences from the training set

Interpolating a memorizing Transformer LM (trained with no dropout to achieve zero training loss) with the original LM improves validation perplexity by only 0.1 points, compared to 1.9 points from kNN-LM, suggesting that implicit memorization in parameters does not generalize as effectively as explicit memory retrieval

The method requires no additional model training; computational overhead consists of a single forward pass to construct the datastore (fraction of one training epoch), approximately 2 hours to build a 103M-entry FAISS index on a single CPU, and approximately 25 minutes to run inference on the validation set when retrieving 1024 keys
